| 7 | 1. SystemVerilog testbench architecture (1/3) | 0:38 | 1. SystemVerilog testbench architecture (1/3). DUT boundary: signals cross via module ports or SystemVerilog interfaces Hierarchy: TB module → DUT instance → optional interface instance Time: #delay, @posedge, and C++ time loop must stay consistent See SYSTEMVERILOG_VERILATOR_INTERACTION.md for compile/sim details Refer to the diagram on the right. |
| 8 | 1. SystemVerilog testbench architecture (2/3) | 0:28 | 1. SystemVerilog testbench architecture (2/3). module2/dut/: RTL blocks under test (registers, FIFOs, state machines) module2/examples/*/: Focused TB patterns (clock, reset, triggers, access) module2/tests/uvm_tests/: UVM wrappers on the same DUTs (bridge to Module 3) Testbench module: Instantiates DUT + interfaces; generates clk/rst |
| 9 | 1. SystemVerilog testbench architecture (3/3) | 0:16 | 1. SystemVerilog testbench architecture (3/3). sim_main.cpp: C++ entry: drives time, calls eval() each cycle |
| 10 | 2. RTL block architecture (example DUTs) | 0:38 | 2. RTL block architecture (example DUTs). Register files — address decode, read/write timing, reset behavior FIFOs — full/empty flags, pointer management, back-pressure State machines — explicit states, transition conditions, output logic Signal flow: clk/rst → sequential logic → status flags → TB monitors Refer to the diagram on the right. |
| 11 | 3. Verilator execution pipeline | 0:38 | 3. Verilator execution pipeline. 1: Makefile — Invokes Verilator with --cc --exe --timing 2: obj_dir/V*.cpp — Generated cycle-accurate C++ model 3: sim_main.cpp — Clock generation and $finish coordination 4: ./obj_dir/V* — Executable; TB prints PASS/FAIL Refer to the diagram on the right. |
| 12 | RTL block diagram (reference) | 0:22 | RTL block diagram (reference). Module 2: DUT hierarchy and signal flow. |

| 15 | How the example runs (toolchain) | 0:32 | How the example runs (toolchain). Match each bullet to files in the repository. Makefile: Verilator + UVM_HOME compile RTL, interface, and test_*.sv UVM phases: build → connect → run_test → report (same pattern as Module 2) Sequence → driver → DUT → monitor → scoreboard (read SCOREBOARD at end) Typical command: make SIM=verilator TEST=test_* (see module2 demo slide) Loopback / hooks connect... |
| 16 | Artifact flow (spec → sim) | 0:28 | Artifact flow (spec → sim). Match each bullet to files in the repository. 1: Makefile — Invokes Verilator with --cc --exe --timing 2: obj_dir/V*.cpp — Generated cycle-accurate C++ model 3: sim_main.cpp — Clock generation and $finish coordination 4: ./obj_dir/V* — Executable; TB prints PASS/FAIL From MODULE2 — execution and artifact layout. |

| 20 | 1. Stimulus and timing strategy | 0:38 | 1. Stimulus and timing strategy. Clock generation: parameterized period/duty; multiple clocks for CDC awareness Reset sequences: assert length, sync deassert, mid-test reset injection Triggers: @posedge, wait(), fork-join for parallel activity Signal access: hierarchical references and interface modport access Refer to the diagram on the right. |
| 21 | 2. Checking and debug workflow (1/2) | 0:38 | 2. Checking and debug workflow (1/2). Self-checking tests compare DUT outputs against expected values each cycle Waveforms (--trace) for debugging timing and reset issues Closure criteria: all directed cases PASS; no $error or timeout ./scripts/module2.sh --all-examples for module-level regression Refer to the diagram on the right. |
| 22 | 2. Checking and debug workflow (2/2) | 0:16 | 2. Checking and debug workflow (2/2). UVM tests reuse same DUTs — compare directed vs phase-based execution in Module 3 |

| 44 | Detail: Best Practices for Testbench Tasks (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Best Practices for Testbench Tasks (2). Default parameter values Type-safe parameters Always synchronize to clock edges Use appropriate delays From MODULE2 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 45 | Detail: Common Pitfalls in Testbench Construction (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Common Pitfalls in Testbench Construction (1). Issue: forever with ~ operator can cause timing issues Solution: Use explicit assignment with delays Issue: Fixed delays don't guarantee clock alignment Solution: Always use @(posedge clk) for synchronization From MODULE2 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 46 | Detail: Common Pitfalls in Testbench Construction (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Common Pitfalls in Testbench Construction (2). Issue: Insufficient reset duration or propagation time Solution: Use configurable duration and add propagation delay Issue: Non-blocking assignments in tasks can cause timing issues Solution: Use blocking assignments (=) in tasks From MODULE2 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 47 | Detail: Performance Considerations (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Performance Considerations (1). Simple clocks: Use always blocks (efficient) Multiple clocks: Each clock needs separate always block Gated clocks: Conditional logic adds minimal overhead Tasks: Slower due to timing simulation overhead From MODULE2 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 48 | Detail: Performance Considerations (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Performance Considerations (2). Functions: Faster (zero-time), but can't use delays Recommendation: Use functions for calculations, tasks for sequences @(posedge clk): Efficient, event-driven #delay: Time-based, requires scheduling From MODULE2 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 49 | Detail: Troubleshooting Guide (1) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Troubleshooting Guide (1). Symptoms: Clock signal stuck at 0 or 1 Causes: always block not executing Delay value too large or zero From MODULE2 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |
| 50 | Detail: Troubleshooting Guide (2) | 0:28 | Protocol and design detail: Troubleshooting Guide (2). Blocking on condition that never becomes true Solutions: Check always block syntax Verify delay values are positive From MODULE2 Topics Covered — protocol/design depth. |

| 85 | 5. Event and Timing Control (8/9) | 0:36 | 5. Event and Timing Control (8/9). | 105 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (9/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (9/23). Type-safe parameters Always synchronize to clock edges Use appropriate delays Avoid race conditions Use assertions for verification |
| 106 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (10/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (10/23). Handle edge cases Encapsulate common sequences Use virtual tasks for polymorphism Create reusable components Issue: forever with ~ operator can cause timing issues |
| 107 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (11/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (11/23). Issue: Fixed delays don't guarantee clock alignment Solution: Always use @(posedge clk) for synchronization Issue: Insufficient reset duration or propagation time Solution: Use configurable duration and add propagation delay Issue: Non-blocking assignments in tasks can cause timing issues |
| 108 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (12/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (12/23). Issue: No way to verify tasks completed successfully Solution: Add completion messages or flags Issue: Wait statement can hang if condition never becomes true Solution: Use fork-join with timeout Simple clocks: Use always blocks (efficient) |
| 109 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (13/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (13/23). Gated clocks: Conditional logic adds minimal overhead Tasks: Slower due to timing simulation overhead Functions: Faster (zero-time), but can't use delays Recommendation: Use functions for calculations, tasks for sequences @(posedge clk): Efficient, event-driven |
| 110 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (14/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (14/23). wait(condition): Polls condition, can be slower fork-join: Creates parallel processes (overhead) Use sparingly: Only when parallel execution is needed Alternative: Sequential execution is faster Symptoms: Clock signal stuck at 0 or 1 |
| 111 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (15/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (15/23). always block not executing Delay value too large or zero Blocking on condition that never becomes true Solutions: Check always block syntax |
| 112 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (16/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (16/23). Use $display to debug clock generation Symptoms: Registers not resetting to expected values Causes: Insufficient reset duration Reset de-asserted too early |
| 113 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (17/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (17/23). Solutions: Increase reset duration Add delay after reset de-assert Verify reset polarity (active-high vs active-low) Symptoms: Signal value doesn't change as expected |
| 114 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (18/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (18/23). Not synchronized to clock Using wrong assignment type Signal not being driven Solutions: Use @(posedge clk) before reading |
| 115 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (19/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (19/23). Verify signal is being driven Symptoms: Inconsistent behavior, timing-dependent failures Causes: Multiple processes accessing same signal Clock and data changing simultaneously |
| 116 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (20/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (20/23). Solutions: Use proper clock synchronization Avoid shared variable access Use non-blocking assignment in sequential logic Symptoms: Code continues before tasks complete |
| 117 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (21/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (21/23). Using join_none instead of join Tasks completing before join Solutions: Use join to wait for all tasks Use join_any to wait for first task |
| 118 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (22/23) | 0:36 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (22/23). Symptoms: Simulation hangs, never completes Causes: Condition never becomes true Signal never assigned Logic error in condition |
| 119 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (23/23) | 0:28 | 7. Functions and Tasks for Testbench Construction (23/23). Add timeout mechanism Verify condition logic Check signal assignments Use $display to debug |

        ## Section narration (edit for TTS)

        - **Design architecture (SystemVerilog testbench architecture, RTL block architecture (example DUTs), Verilator execution pipeline):** Walk through the block diagram, then relate each block to files under module2/examples/.
- **Execution:** Explain make run / UVM make steps, then walk the artifact table and directed-test sequence slide by slide.
- **Verification (Stimulus and timing strategy, Checking and debug workflow):** Explain what stimulus is applied, what is checked, and what is intentionally out of scope.
- **Syllabus:** Cover 8 topic section(s) — pause on protocol timing and signals.
- **Before exercises:** Ask learners to recall the learning outcomes slide; they should explain each bullet in their own words.
- **Hands-on:** Run module2/EXAMPLES.md labs; narrate expected PASS lines.

        ## Notes

        - Slides from **Before You Start**, **Design Architecture**, **Verification & Testing Methods**, **Topics Covered**, **EXAMPLES.md**, and **Learning Outcomes**.
        - Full detail: `docs/MODULE2.md` and `module2/EXAMPLES.md`.
